hero_summary: Farahnak belongs to the Sikah family, and its tonic is Iraq (B half-flat), not Sikah (E half-flat). It is commonly described through Jins Sikah on Iraq, followed by Jins Jiharkah on Dukah and Jins Rast on Nawa.
short_identity: A Sikah-family maqam resolving on Iraq (B half-flat), distinguished by a middle Jiharkah layer and an upper Rast layer.

quick_facts.upper_jins: Jins Ajam on Dukah / Jins Rast on Nawa in practical block naming and explanation
quick_facts.character: A composite Iraq-based Sikah maqam with a strong upper expansion
quick_facts.common_start: It begins from the lower Sikah color on Iraq, rises through Dukah, then opens into Rast on Nawa before returning to the tonic

sayr.summary: Farahnak belongs clearly to the Sikah family. Its tonic is Iraq (B half-flat), and comparative references place it alongside Iraq, Rahat al-Arwah, and Basta Nikar among the maqamat built on Iraq within the Sikah sphere. In concise Arabic reference style it may be described as Jins Sikah on Iraq, followed by Jins Jiharkah on Dukah (D), then Jins Rast on Nawa (G).
resting_tones: Iraq (B half-flat): the tonic and final resting point
Dukah (D): an important middle point because it carries the Jiharkah layer
Nawa (G): an upper point of rest where Rast becomes audible
motion_notes: Its character is not captured by a plain scale formula, but by how the performer activates Sikah on Iraq, then Jiharkah on Dukah, then Rast on Nawa
References also point to a descending variant inside the maqam, showing that its sayr is not confined to one rigid path
If the lower Iraq-based Sikah color is blurred, the maqam may be mistaken for something closer to Ajam or to another family
The performer must preserve the Sikah-family tonic while opening the upper region clearly
common_path: Establish the tonic on Iraq (B half-flat)
Reveal the middle Jiharkah layer on Dukah (D)
Open the upper Rast layer on Nawa (G)
Return to Iraq with a firm cadence

common_modulations: Iraq and Farahnak both resolve on Iraq (B half-flat) within the Sikah family, but Iraq has a different structural presentation, whereas Farahnak is characterized by the activation of Jiharkah and then Rast in the upper path
Basta Nikar is also built on Iraq inside the Sikah family, but its upper-middle structure is Saba on Dukah, while Farahnak is not Saba-based there; rather, it is Jiharkah in the middle and Rast above
Sikah and Huzam belong to the same broader family, but their usual tonic is not Iraq in this sense, and Farahnak has a distinctly different upper ordering
